statement.en: Addressing Image Authenticity When Cameras Use Generative AI
statement.summary: The ability of generative AI (GenAI) methods to photorealistically alter camera images has raised awareness about the authenticity of images shared online. Interestingly, images captured directly by our cameras are considered authentic and faithful. However, with the increasing integration of deep-learning modules into cameras' capture-time hardware -- namely, the image signal processor (ISP) -- there is now a potential for hallucinated content in images directly output by our cameras. Hallucinated capture-time image content is typically benign, such as enhanced edges or texture, but in certain operations, such as AI-based digital zoom or low-light image enhancement, hallucinations can potentially alter the semantics and interpretation of the image content. As a result, users may not reali

proposer: Umar Masud, Abhijith Punnappurath, Luxi Zhao et al.

reiTyping.dfumt8Reason: The paper establishes that camera-native GenAI integration creates a genuine technical threat (TRUE aspect: hallucination occurs in ISP pipelines), but simultaneously challenges the intuition that camera-output images are inherently authentic (FALSE aspect: traditional assumption breaks down). The axiom itself is dialectical—it refutes prior belief while confirming new risk.
